1:
“Let not your heart be troubled: ye believe in God, believe also in me.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s tender consolation of disciples shaken by the news of his departure, the betrayal, and Peter’s foretold denial. The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene: Ellicott notes the “unfortunate” chapter-division, for these words follow hard upon the prophecy of the denial (John 13:38), which “may well have brought troubled hearts.” Robertson observes that “heart” here is “not the physical organ but the seat of spiritual life,” “the focus of the religious life,” and that the disciples’ hearts “were tossed like waves in the wind.” Lightfoot adds that beyond the grief of parting lay the collapse of their hopes — they had looked for a restored kingdom and earthly triumph (Acts 1:6; Luke 24:21), and now their Master speaks of dying. Henry marks how Christ “took notice” of the trouble he read in their faces (John 13:22), “acquainted with all our secret undiscovered sorrows.”

The Latin Augustine fastens on the deity implied in the remedy. “That they might not as men be afraid of death, and so be troubled, He comforts them by affirming Himself also to be God”: “Believe in God, believe also in me,” for the second “would not follow, unless Christ were God.” “You are in fear for this form of a servant,” he says; “let not your heart be troubled — the form of God shall raise it up.” The Eastern Chrysostom adds that faith in Christ “and in the Father that begat Me is more powerful than anything that shall come upon you.” Calvin, for the Reformed, notes that Christ does not merely exhort to steadfastness but “informs them where they must go to obtain courage; that is, by faith,” for “a contest so arduous” awaited them — soon they would see him “hanging on the cross,” a spectacle of seeming despair. The witnesses thus find in this opening verse the theme of the whole discourse: against every trouble of heart Christ sets faith — faith in God, and equally in himself as God — so that the disciples may meet his death not with despair but with trust in the One who, in the form of a servant, is yet their God.
2:
“In my Father’s house are many mansions: if it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.” The witnesses dwell on the picture of heaven and debate the meaning of “many mansions.” The grammatical-historical commentators take “my Father’s house” of heaven, “the habitation of God” (Ellicott; cf. 2 Corinthians 5:1). Robertson explains “mansions” (monai, from menō, to abide) as “abiding places, resting-places,” and calls this “Christ’s picture of heaven… the most precious one that we possess — our heavenly home with the Father and with Jesus.” On whether the “many” implies degrees of glory, the commentators divide: Clarke reads it of “various degrees of glory, suited to the various capacities and attainments of my followers,” alluding to the many chambers of the temple, while Calvin expressly resists this — Christ “says that the mansions are many, not that they are different” — taking it only of room enough for all. Lightfoot connects “I go to prepare a place” with the ark that “went before them to search out a resting place” (Numbers 10:33).

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the comfort the verse was meant to give: Christ “does not go away in such a manner as to remain separate from them, since there is room for them also in the heavenly kingdom,” removing “the suspicion… that, when Christ ascended to the Father, he left his disciples on earth without taking any farther notice of them.” Wesley reads the “many” of the vastness of the welcome — “enough to receive both the holy angels, and your predecessors in the faith, and all that now believe, and a great multitude which no man can number.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the great promise that turns the disciples’ grief at his going into hope: he departs not to abandon them but to prepare their place in the Father’s house, where there is room enough for all his own. The very fact that he goes is for their sake; his leaving is the making ready of their home.
3:
“And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again, and receive you unto myself; that where I am, there ye may be also.” The witnesses dwell on the promise of Christ’s return and its blessed end. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the conditional “if I go” implies no uncertainty but “expresses that the fact is in the region of the future, which is clear to Him” (Ellicott; Robertson reads the third-class condition). On “I will come again,” the commentators offer several senses: Robertson takes it as “a definite promise of the second coming of Christ,” the present “I come again” being futuristic; Clarke refers it to his coming “after my resurrection… by my grace and the effusion of my Spirit”; Ellicott lists the readings — “the resurrection; the death of individual disciples; the spiritual presence… in the Church; the coming again of the Lord in the Parousia of the last day.” The phrase “receive you unto myself,” Robertson renders “I shall take you along to my own home” (John 13:36), a promise “fulfilled in death for all believers who die before the Second Coming.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, takes the coming chiefly of “the last day of judgment, when he will, at length, come to assemble his followers,” though he grants that “Christ dwells with us and in us by his Spirit” daily. The witnesses agree that, whatever the precise mode of his coming, the goal is one: “that where I am, there ye may be also” — the everlasting union of the disciples with their Lord. This, all the commentators find, is the crown of the promise. Christ goes to prepare the place not that his own may dwell there apart from him, but that they may be with him; the heaven he opens is, above all, his presence. The witnesses thus read the verse as the sweetest consolation of the discourse: the departing Lord pledges to return and to gather his people to himself, so that the parting now grieving them is but the prelude to an eternal togetherness — where he is, there they shall be.
4:
“And whither I go ye know, and the way ye know.” The witnesses note Christ’s assertion that the disciples already possessed the knowledge they thought they lacked. The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the better reading: Ellicott prefers “whither I go, ye know the way” — “ye know that I am the way to the Father, whither I am going” — adding that “they did not, indeed, fully know this, but the means of knowing it was within their reach,” and that “to know our ignorance is the first step” toward knowledge. Henry presses both senses: “you may know — it is none of the secret things… the word is nigh you” (Romans 10:6–8); and “you do know,” though imperfectly. Barnes notes that Christ had so often foretold his death, resurrection, and ascension (Matthew 16:21; Luke 18:31) that “they could not but understand it”; Robertson hears “a definite allusion to the puzzle of Peter” (John 13:36), “the path to the Father’s house now plain.”

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, reflects that the earlier words about the mansions are best understood from the later answer to Thomas — “those mansions already existed in predestination, and are also being prepared through the purifying by faith of the hearts” of those who will dwell there. Calvin, for the Reformed, gathers the two things the disciples must hold: that Christ’s death “is not a destruction, but a passage to the Father,” and that they too must follow the same way to share his glory. The witnesses thus find the verse a gentle challenge: Christ credits the disciples with more knowledge than their fear allows them to feel, that he may draw out the confession of their ignorance and so teach them more fully. Their half-knowledge of the goal and the way is real, and ready to be made plain; and the very next exchange, in which Thomas owns that they do not know, will draw from Christ the greatest of his self-disclosures — that he himself is the way.

The witnesses note the kindness of Christ’s assumption. Rather than upbraid the disciples for their slowness, he credits them with knowing what their fear had clouded, and so invites them to take heart. It is the way of the good Teacher to call out the knowledge already given before adding more; and the disciples, told they ‘know,’ are gently led to discover both how much they have been taught and how much they still need to learn.
5:
“Thomas saith unto him, Lord, we know not whither thou goest; and how can we know the way?” The witnesses find in Thomas’s blunt question both honest perplexity and the occasion of a great answer. The grammatical-historical commentators read it as the same literal-minded honesty that marks Thomas elsewhere (cf. John 11:16; John 20:25): he takes Christ’s “whither” of a place and his “way” of a road, and, not grasping the spiritual sense, confesses that they know neither the destination nor the route. Gill, Barnes, and Ellicott note that Thomas speaks for the others’ confusion as well, voicing aloud the bewilderment the discourse had stirred; his frankness, though it betrays his dullness, at least brings the difficulty into the open where Christ can resolve it. The Eastern Chrysostom reads in the interruption the disciples’ anxious love, unwilling to be left without knowing where their Master goes.

The witnesses dwell on the value of Thomas’s very ignorance. Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others observe that the honest avowal “we know not” is the door to instruction; had Thomas pretended to understand, he would have shut himself out from the answer. By owning his perplexity he draws from Christ the clearest of all his self-revelations. The witnesses thus read the verse as the foil to v.6: the disciple’s twofold question — the whither and the way — will be met by Christ’s twofold (indeed threefold) claim, that he is himself the way, and the truth, and the life, and the only access to the Father. Thomas’s honest confusion, like Peter’s earlier question (John 13:36) and Philip’s coming request (v.8), becomes the means by which the deepest things of the discourse are drawn out; the slow questions of the disciples are the occasions of the Lord’s greatest words.

The witnesses observe that Thomas’s candour, for all its dullness, is better than a feigned understanding. He will not pretend to a knowledge he lacks, and his honesty opens the way for the truth. So the verse commends the frankness that brings our ignorance to Christ rather than hiding it; for the disciple who confesses ‘we know not’ is the one to whom the Lord can say, ‘I am the way.’ The slow heart that asks honestly is nearer the light than the proud heart that will not ask.
6:
“Jesus saith unto him, I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me.” The witnesses receive this as one of the supreme self-revelations of the Gospel, drawn out by Thomas’s confusion. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the threefold claim. Robertson observes that “either of these statements is profound enough to stagger any one, but here all three together overwhelm Thomas”: Christ is “the way to God and the only way,” “the personification of truth, the centre of life.” Ellicott marks the emphatic “I” — “I, and none besides Me” — and that “the way” is set first, reversing Thomas’s order, since Christ is the sole “means through which men can approach to the Father” (cf. 1 Timothy 2:5). Clarke unfolds the three: he is the Way “by his doctrine, his example, his sacrifice, his Spirit”; the Truth “in opposition to all false religions” and as the substance of the Law’s shadow; the Life “that animates all who seek and serve him,” to be enjoyed eternally at the way’s end. Wesley neatly maps the three onto the disciples’ questions: to the question of the way, “I am the way”; of knowledge, “the truth”; of the whither, “the life.”

Lightfoot notes the Hebrew idiom by which “the way, the truth, and the life” may mean “the true and living way,” and the Eastern Origen marvels at the “plurality of names of good things” by which the Son of God is announced — life, light, truth — “how many good things Jesus is.” Calvin, for the Reformed, observes that Christ checks Thomas’s curiosity about heaven and “dwells on a subject far more necessary”: not what his condition will be when departed, but how men come to the Father. The witnesses thus find the verse the heart of the chapter and of the gospel: against every searching for God by other roads, Christ sets himself as the one Way, Truth, and Life. He does not merely show the way, speak the truth, and give the life; he is them, in his own person, so that to have him is to have access to the Father, the truth that reveals him, and the life that unites us to him. And the exclusive clause — “no man cometh unto the Father, but by me” — shuts every other door, making Christ not one way among many but the only way to God.
7:
“If ye had known me, ye should have known my Father also: and from henceforth ye know him, and have seen him.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s lesson that to know him truly is to know the Father. The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the grammar. Ellicott notes the emphasis falls not on “Me” but on “known,” and that the two Greek verbs differ — “to know by observation” and “to know” more inwardly. Robertson reads the past-perfect condition (“if ye had really come to know me”) and the inchoative present that follows — “ye are beginning to know the Father from now on” — for “Thomas and the rest had not really come to know Jesus, much as they loved him.” Barnes guards the sense: Christ does not deny they are his disciples, but says “they had not a full and accurate knowledge of his character,” still clinging to “Jewish notions respecting a temporal Messiah.” Clarke grounds the saying in the unity of Father and Son: “if ye had known me, ye should have known my Father, because I and the Father are one” (John 10:30).

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the rebuke and the remedy together. Christ “blames the disciples for not acknowledging” the God who stands before them: it is “a foolish and pernicious curiosity, when men, not satisfied with him, attempt to go to God by indirect and crooked paths.” They “profess to be earnest in seeking God; and when he presents himself before our eyes, we are blind” — seeking “above the clouds him whom they do not deign to acknowledge as present.” Augustine, for the Latin fathers, insists the words must agree with what precedes, where Christ showed that the way they knew “was Himself.” The witnesses thus find the verse pressing home the truth of v.6: there is no knowledge of God apart from Christ, and no need to look beyond him for the Father. To have come to know the Son is already to have known and seen the Father; and the disciples, who have had Christ before them all this while, are gently chided for not perceiving that in him the Father has been with them all along.
8:
“Philip saith unto him, Lord, shew us the Father, and it sufficeth us.” The witnesses read Philip’s request as a craving for some visible theophany, missing the truth Christ has just spoken. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Philip “catches at the word ‘seen’” (v.7) and “thinks of some revelation of the glory of God” such as Moses received (Exodus 33:18), or the vision on the Mount of Transfiguration (Ellicott, Robertson). Clarke notes the demand “was similar to that made by Moses,” who “wished to see the glory of God,” and observes that Philip, “one of the first-called disciples,” had not been on the holy mount. Barnes grants that “it was right to desire evidence that Jesus was the Messiah,” but “such evidence had been afforded abundantly in the miracles and teaching of Jesus, and that should have sufficed them.” Lightfoot puts the request in Jewish terms: as Israel “saw God in his glory” when the law was given through Moses, so Philip asks that the bringer of the new law “shew us the Father… and it will suffice us.”

The Latin Hilary observes that “a declaration so new startled Philip”: he “did not deny He could be seen, but wished to be shown him,” not “with his bodily eyes” only, but that the Father might “be made manifest.” The Eastern Chrysostom is sharper, reading in the persistent request a slowness “shameless” in setting itself against a truth so plainly told. Calvin, for the Reformed, turns the rebuke on every reader: it seems “absurd that the Apostles should offer so many objections,” yet “there is not one of their faults… that may not be charged on us as well”; “we profess to be earnest in seeking God, and when he presents himself before our eyes, we are blind.” The witnesses thus find in Philip’s longing both a true desire and a deep misunderstanding. He rightly counts the sight of the Father the one thing that would suffice; he wrongly looks for it past Christ, in some further manifestation, when the Father is fully revealed in the Son standing before him. His request becomes the occasion of Christ’s plainest word on his unity with the Father.
9:
“Jesus saith unto him, Have I been so long time with you, and yet hast thou not known me, Philip? he that hath seen me hath seen the Father.” The witnesses hear in this both a sorrowful rebuke and the chapter’s plainest assertion of Christ’s deity. The grammatical-historical commentators note the pathos of “so long time”: for more than three years Philip had companied with Jesus, seen his miracles, heard his teaching, and yet had “not recognised” him (Ellicott, who finds “a tone of sadness and of warning… the loneliness of a holiness and greatness which is not understood”). Barnes observes that after “so many proofs of his divine mission” there was no need that God “should visibly manifest himself to them.” On the great clause “he that hath seen me hath seen the Father,” the commentators find an unmistakable claim to Godhead. Clarke asks pointedly: “Could any creature say these words? Do they not evidently imply that Christ declared himself to his disciples to be the everlasting God?” Robertson reads it as plainly: to have seen Christ is to have seen the Father, for the Father is revealed in the Son.

The witnesses dwell on the force of the saying. It does not mean that the Father’s essence is visible to bodily eyes — the Father remains invisible (John 1:18) — but that in the person, words, and works of Christ the Father is so fully manifested that to know the Son is to behold the Father. Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find here the answer to Philip’s request: he asked for a sight of the Father, and Christ replies that the sight he craves has been before him all along, in the Son who is the image of the invisible God. The witnesses thus read the verse as the heart of the discourse on the Father and the Son. Philip need look no further than Christ; the theophany he desired is not a future flash of glory but the very face of Jesus, in whom the Father is seen. To have looked upon Christ in faith is to have looked upon God.
10:
“Believest thou not that I am in the Father, and the Father in me? the words that I speak unto you I speak not of myself: but the Father that dwelleth in me, he doeth the works.” The witnesses take this as Christ’s explanation of how seeing him is seeing the Father — the mutual indwelling of Father and Son. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the reciprocal formula, “I in the Father, and the Father in me,” the deep unity by which the Son perfectly reveals the Father. The proof Christ offers is twofold: his words are “not of myself” — not self-originated but the Father’s — and his works are the Father’s, “the Father that dwelleth in me, he doeth the works.” Barnes and Robertson note that this union is so complete that the words and deeds of Jesus are the very words and deeds of God; the Father abiding in him is the source of all. Ellicott reads it as the ground of the preceding claim: because Father and Son so indwell one another, to behold the one is to behold the other.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, finds here the great mystery of the divine persons’ co-inherence — Father and Son distinct, yet each in the other, one God. Calvin, for the Reformed, presses that Christ points the disciples to his words and works as the visible evidence of this hidden unity: they cannot see the indwelling directly, but they hear the Father speaking in the Son’s teaching and see the Father working in the Son’s miracles. The witnesses thus find in the verse both doctrine and evidence. The doctrine is the mutual indwelling — the Son in the Father and the Father in the Son — which grounds the claim that he who has seen Christ has seen the Father. The evidence is the words and the works, in which the Father, dwelling in the Son, makes himself known. So Philip, and every believer, is taught to find the invisible Father in the audible words and visible works of Jesus, and to believe, on their witness, the unity of the Son with the Father which the eye of sense cannot reach.
11:
“Believe me that I am in the Father, and the Father in me: or else believe me for the very works’ sake.” The witnesses note that Christ now condescends to the disciples’ weakness, offering two grounds of faith. The grammatical-historical commentators observe the order: first he asks them to believe his bare word concerning the mutual indwelling (v.10) — to trust his own testimony to his unity with the Father; but if that is too high for their faith, he stoops to a lower ground, “believe me for the very works’ sake.” The miracles he has wrought, the commentators agree, are God’s own seal upon his claim, evidence that none but God could give, so that even those slow to receive the doctrine on his word may be brought to it by the witness of his deeds (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson; cf. John 10:38). Wesley notes the gentleness of the appeal — Christ meets a weak faith where it is, and leads it upward.

The witnesses dwell on the pastoral wisdom of the verse. Christ does not despise a faith that needs the help of evidence; he provides it, pointing to the works as a ladder by which the disciples may climb to belief in the unseen unity of Father and Son. Cyril of Jerusalem and the others find here the proper use of Christ’s miracles — not ends in themselves, but signs that authenticate his word and lead to faith in his person. The witnesses thus read the verse as a model of how Christ deals with the weak in faith. He sets the highest ground first — his own word — but does not leave behind those who cannot yet rise to it; for them he points to the works, that, believing first on the evidence of what they have seen him do, they may come at last to believe on his word alone. The deeds are given to bring the doubting to the doctrine, and the doctrine is that the Son and the Father are one.

The witnesses observe that the verse honours both faith and evidence in their proper order. The highest faith rests on Christ’s own word, needing no further proof; yet Christ does not scorn the faith that must lean on his works, but graciously supplies it. So the believer is neither rebuked for wanting evidence nor left to rest in it, but is led from the witness of the works to the trust that takes Christ at his word — that he is in the Father, and the Father in him.
12:
“Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that believeth on me, the works that I do shall he do also; and greater works than these shall he do; because I go unto my Father.” The witnesses dwell on the startling promise and debate the nature of the “greater works.” The grammatical-historical commentators agree the promise looked chiefly to the apostles (Gill, Barnes), who would work signs and wonders by Christ’s power. On the “greater,” the commentators divide. Robertson takes it of quantity, not quality: “not necessarily greater miracles… but greater in quantity,” as at Pentecost and on Paul’s mission tours. The Latin Augustine and Wesley point to the conversion of souls: Wesley grants that one apostle healed by his shadow (Acts 5:15) and another by handkerchiefs (Acts 19:12), “but the converting one sinner is a greater work than all these.” Clarke reads it of greater miracles wrought through his almighty power. Ellicott grounds the promise in union: “he that by faith becomes one with the Son… shall himself become an instrument through which God… shall carry into effect His own works.”

The witnesses note the reason Christ gives — “because I go unto my Father.” The Eastern Chrysostom marvels that Christ not only does great works but will “give others the power of working them.” The going to the Father, all agree, is the condition of the greater works, for from the Father he will send the Spirit, by whom the church would spread the gospel through all the world (Wesley, Robertson). Calvin, for the Reformed, sees in the promise the completing of the disciples’ consolation: Christ will not be the less present for his absence, but, departing to the Father, will work through their hands works greater than any they had seen. The witnesses thus find in the verse a promise that turns the grief of his going into hope: his departure is not the end of his working but its enlargement, for the believing church, indwelt by his Spirit, will carry the gospel further and gather more souls than his own earthly ministry — the greater works being above all the worldwide harvest of conversion that his ascension and the gift of the Spirit make possible.
13:
“And whatsoever ye shall ask in my name, that will I do, that the Father may be glorified in the Son.” The witnesses unfold the great promise of prevailing prayer and its conditions. The grammatical-historical commentators note both the width and the limitation. The width is “whatsoever” — Barnes extends it to all Christians, “if what they ask is in faith and according to the will of God” (James 1:6; 1 John 5:14). The limitation is “in my name”: Ellicott and Robertson explain this as praying “as My representatives on earth,” in dependence on Christ and in accord with his will, not a mere formula appended to a request. Robertson marks this as “the first mention of his ‘name’ as the open sesame to the Father’s will” (cf. John 16:23), and notes that “the Father answers prayers, but so does the Son,” for here Christ himself says “that will I do.” The end of all is doxological — “that the Father may be glorified in the Son.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the Mediatorship the verse implies: “he will be the Author of all that shall be done by the hands of the Apostles,” discharging “the office of Mediator… in whose name men ought to pray to the Father.” The witnesses dwell on the comfort and the discipline of the promise together. It is comfort, for the departing Lord assures his own that, though absent in body, he will answer their prayers and supply their need by his own hand. It is discipline, for the “in my name” shapes prayer to his will and his glory: we may ask whatsoever we will, but only as those who pray in his name, seeking what he seeks, that the Father may be glorified in the Son. The witnesses thus find in the verse the charter of Christian prayer: bold in its scope, exact in its condition, and sure in its answer — the Son himself pledging to do what is asked in his name, to the Father’s glory.

The witnesses note that the promise binds prayer to the glory of God. We do not ask in Christ’s name to gratify our own desires merely, but that the Father may be glorified in the Son; and prayer so offered cannot fail, for its very aim is the end God himself seeks. The verse thus teaches both the boldness and the holiness of true prayer — bold to ask all things, holy to ask them only in his name and for his glory.
14:
“If ye shall ask any thing in my name, I will do it.” The witnesses treat this as a deliberate, strengthening repetition of the promise of v.13. The grammatical-historical commentators note the emphasis: Ellicott marks the stress on “I” — “I, on My part, will do it” — and Gill suggests the words are “repeated by Christ, the more to strengthen and confirm the faith of his disciples” in the certainty of answered prayer. Robertson observes the well-attested reading “ask me anything in my name,” which, if genuine, “here is direct prayer to Jesus taught,” as practised by Stephen (Acts 7:59) and in the Revelation (Revelation 22:20) — so that the Son is not only the one in whose name we pray to the Father, but himself the one prayed to and the one who answers.

Calvin, for the Reformed, finds the repetition far from useless. “All see and feel that they are unworthy to approach God,” and so men either “rashly and haughtily address God” or, recalling their unworthiness, “contrive for themselves various expedients.” Against both, Christ “holds out to us one Mediator only, by whom” God “is willing to be approached.” The witnesses thus read the verse as a gracious reassurance to a faith slow to believe its own access. By saying the same thing twice, Christ presses home that prayer in his name is sure of its answer, and fixes the one ground of confidence — his name, his mediation — by which the unworthy may come boldly to God. The disciple is to ask nothing in his own merit, and everything in Christ’s name; and the Son who bids him ask pledges, with double assurance, that he himself will do it.

The witnesses draw comfort from the doubling of the pledge. A faith slow to believe that it may have such access is met by Christ’s patient reassurance, said once and then again, that whatsoever is asked in his name he will do. The repetition is not idle but pastoral, fixing in the disciples’ hearts a confidence that no unworthiness need shake, since the ground of answered prayer is not their merit but his name.
15:
“If ye love me, keep my commandments.” The witnesses take this as the hinge between the promises of prayer and the gift of the Spirit, making obedience the proof of love. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that love to Christ is shown not by feeling or profession but by obedience: Barnes — “do not show your love by grief at my departure merely, or by profession, but by obedience,” for “mere profession is no proof of love.” Robertson reads the third-class condition, “if ye keep on loving me,” with the future “ye will keep,” and notes that “continued love prevents disobedience” (cf. 1 John 2:5). The Eastern Chrysostom connects it to the foregoing: lest they think “simply asking would be enough,” Christ adds the condition of obedience; and to their grief at his going he answers, “to love Me is not to be troubled, but to keep My commandments.” Ellicott marks the emphatic “my” — “the commandments which ye have received from Me,” perhaps those of this last discourse especially.

Calvin, for the Reformed, discerns a fault even in the disciples’ love: it was “true and sincere,” yet had “some superstition mixed with it,” for “it was very foolish in them to wish to keep him in the world.” So Christ “bids them direct their love to another end” — to employing themselves in the work he had given them. The witnesses thus find the verse correcting a sentimental devotion and grounding love in action. Of those who think they love Christ, Calvin notes, “there are very few who honour him as they ought.” The love Christ seeks is not a grief that would cling to his bodily presence, nor a warmth of feeling that ends in itself, but an obedience that keeps his commandments. And this obedient love, the next verses show, is the channel of the promised Spirit: it is to those who love him in this way — by keeping his word — that the Father, at the Son’s asking, will give the Comforter to abide for ever.

The witnesses press the searching simplicity of the test. Love that does not obey is no love at all, whatever its professions or its tears; and obedience that does not flow from love is mere servility. Christ joins the two inseparably: ‘if ye love me, keep my commandments.’ So the verse rebukes both a loveless legalism and a disobedient sentiment, and sets the mark of true devotion in a loving obedience that does the will of the Lord it loves.
16:
“And I will pray the Father, and he shall give you another Comforter, that he may abide with you for ever.” The witnesses receive this as the great promise of the Holy Spirit, the Paraclete. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on each term. Christ here speaks “as Mediator” (Gill), praying the Father to give the Spirit. The word “Comforter” (paraklētos) Robertson explains as “called to one’s side” — an Advocate, Helper, Comforter; and “another” (allos) means “another of the same kind” as Christ himself, so that the Spirit is no lesser substitute but a divine Comforter like the Son. The contrast is with Christ’s own departing bodily presence: he had been their Paraclete in the flesh for a little while, but the Spirit “shall abide with you for ever” (Robertson, Ellicott), never to be taken away as Christ now would be.

The witnesses dwell on the comfort of this “for ever.” Where Christ’s bodily presence was temporary and local, the Spirit’s indwelling would be perpetual and universal, with the church to the end of the age. Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find here the answer to the disciples’ grief: they sorrow to lose their Master’s presence, and he promises a presence that will never leave them — another Comforter, of his own kind, sent at his asking and the Father’s gift. The witnesses thus read the verse as the turning of the discourse from departure to abiding. Christ goes, but does not leave them orphans (v.18); in his stead, and by his prayer, the Father gives the Spirit, who takes up a residence more intimate and more lasting than the bodily presence now ending. The Comforter promised is the abiding gift that makes the absence of Christ not a bereavement but an exchange — his nearer presence by the Spirit, with his own for ever.

The witnesses observe the tender logic of the promise. Christ does not leave his sorrowing disciples to face his absence alone, but undertakes himself to pray the Father, and pledges the gift of a Comforter who will never depart. Where his own presence was for a season, the Spirit’s is for ever; and so the loss they dread is turned into a greater nearness. The promise of the abiding Paraclete is the heart of the consolation Christ gives against the grief of his going.
17:
“Even the Spirit of truth; whom the world cannot receive, because it seeth him not, neither knoweth him: but ye know him; for he dwelleth with you, and shall be in you.” The witnesses unfold the Spirit’s name, the world’s incapacity, and the disciples’ privilege. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “the Spirit of truth” as the one who teaches, guides into, and testifies to the truth that is Christ (cf. John 16:13); Bede adds that the title shows him to be Christ’s own Spirit, even as his being given by the Father shows him the Father’s — “the Holy Ghost proceeds both from the Father and from the Son.” On the world’s inability, the commentators note that the world “cannot receive” him because it “seeth him not, neither knoweth him”: lacking the spiritual sense, it has no capacity for a Spirit it cannot perceive (Gill, Barnes). Gregory observes that “the Holy Spirit kindles in every one in whom He dwells the desire of things invisible,” which “worldly minds” that “love only things visible” cannot share.

On the disciples’ side, the witnesses mark the present and the future: “he dwelleth with you, and shall be in you.” Robertson and Ellicott note the change from “with” (par’ hymin) to “in” (en hymin): the Spirit was already with them in the presence of Christ and in his gifts, but would come to dwell within them at Pentecost. Calvin, for the Reformed, presses that the world’s incapacity is not of nature only but of will — it will not have the Spirit because it loves the darkness — while the disciples know him because grace has opened their eyes. The witnesses thus find in the verse a sharp division between the world and the church at the point of the Spirit. The world cannot receive him, for it neither sees nor knows him; the disciples both know him now and shall be indwelt by him hereafter. The Comforter promised is the Spirit of truth, hidden from the world but given to Christ’s own, to dwell not merely beside them but within them, the abiding presence of God in the believing heart.
18:
“I will not leave you comfortless: I will come to you.” The witnesses dwell on the tender word “orphans” and the promise of Christ’s own return. The grammatical-historical commentators correct the rendering: Ellicott and Robertson note the Greek is “orphans” (orphanous, bereft children), not “comfortless” — a word that wrongly suggests a link with “Comforter.” Since Christ had just called them “little children” (John 13:33), it is natural that, leaving them, he should not leave them fatherless. Gill notes that “Christ stands in the relation of a Father to his people,” so the disciples might fear to be “left as children without a father”; against this Christ promises, “I will come to you.” Robertson allows the word may also mean “helpless” — without the other Paraclete — but the parental sense fits the context. Henry catches the homely comfort: as parting friends ask to hear from one another, so Christ engages that “out of sight they should not be out of mind.”

The Latin Augustine guards the promise from misunderstanding: “lest any should suppose that the Lord was to give the Spirit, as it were, in place of Himself,” so that he himself would not be present, “He added… I will come to you.” Christ both sends the Spirit and comes himself. Hilary reads the coming as Christ’s presence in his own “by the mystery of the Sacrament,” the union of John 6:56. Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the lesson of dependence: this passage “shows what men are… when they have been deprived of the protection of the Spirit” — orphans, “incapable of governing themselves” — and “the only remedy… is, if Christ govern us by his Spirit.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the answer to the disciples’ fear of abandonment. Christ goes, but not to forsake; he will come to them — in his resurrection, in the Spirit, in his abiding presence — so that they shall never be fatherless. The Comforter promised does not replace Christ but conveys him; and the orphaned disciples are pledged the return of the Father who is their Lord.
19:
“Yet a little while, and the world seeth me no more; but ye see me: because I live, ye shall live also.” The witnesses draw out the contrast between the world’s blindness and the disciples’ sight, and the great guarantee of life. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that “a little while” brings the cross, after which “the world seeth me no more” — to bodily eyes, until the judgment (Gill, Barnes) — but the disciples “shall continue to see me by faith, even when the world cannot” (Barnes). Ellicott takes “ye see me” of “the spiritual presence of the Paraclete,” perhaps first fulfilled in the appearances of the forty days, which were “steps in the education… leading the disciples from a trust in the physical to a trust in the spiritual presence.” Robertson marks the emphatic “ye” against “the blind, unseeing world,” and calls “because I live, ye shall live also” “our blessed guarantee of immortal, eternal life, the continued living of Jesus,” who is “the surety of a better covenant” (Hebrews 7:22).

Clarke and Wesley unfold the link of life. Clarke: “as surely as I shall rise from the dead, so shall ye. My resurrection shall be the proof and pledge of yours”; and because Christ lives “a life of intercession… ye shall live a life of grace and peace here, and a life of glory hereafter.” Calvin, for the Reformed, presses that the disciples’ continued seeing depends on faith, not sense: “we must not judge of his presence or his absence according to carnal perception, but… employ the eyes of faith.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both a division and a pledge. The division is between the world, which loses sight of Christ at the cross, and the disciples, who behold him by faith and the Spirit. The pledge is the believer’s life bound to Christ’s own: because he lives — risen and ever-living — his own shall live too, the life of faith now and of glory hereafter. The continued living of the Lord is the surety of the everlasting life of his people.
20:
“At that day ye shall know that I am in my Father, and ye in me, and I in you.” The witnesses unfold the threefold union and the day of its disclosure. The grammatical-historical commentators take “that day” of the new era opened by the resurrection and Pentecost: Robertson calls it “the New Dispensation of the Holy Spirit,” to which “chapters 1 to 3 of Acts bear eloquent witness”; Ellicott reads the day of the Comforter’s gift, “in whom Christ shall come to them,” though “the words hold good of every spiritual quickening.” The knowledge promised is the deep apprehension of the mutual indwelling — “that I am in my Father” (the Son’s unity with the Father, John 10:38), “and ye in me, and I in you” (the believer’s union with Christ). Barnes marks the intimacy: “we are most intimately and indissolubly united,” and “there is a union between us which can never be severed.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, would not limit “that day” to Pentecost alone, but takes it of “the uninterrupted course… of a single day, from the time when Christ exerted the power of his Spirit till the last resurrection” — the believers began to know then, “but it was a feeble beginning, because the Spirit had not yet wrought so powerfully.” The point of the words, he adds, is “to show that we cannot, by indolent speculation, know what is the sacred and mystical union between us and him,” but only by the Spirit’s teaching. The witnesses thus find in the verse the grand chain of union that the Spirit reveals: the Son in the Father, the believers in the Son, the Son in the believers — so that the disciple is drawn, through Christ, into the very life of God. This knowledge is not a matter of clever reasoning but of the Spirit’s illumination, given “at that day,” when the risen and ascended Lord pours out the Comforter who makes the union known and felt.

The witnesses note that the verse gathers the chapter’s teaching on union into a single chain. The Son is in the Father; the believers are in the Son; the Son is in the believers — so that through Christ the disciple is drawn into the very fellowship of the Godhead. And this is not a truth to be reasoned out, but one to be known by experience when the Spirit is given. The promise of ‘that day’ is the promise that what the disciples now half-grasp they shall then know with the certainty of those in whom God dwells.
21:
“He that hath my commandments, and keepeth them, he it is that loveth me: and he that loveth me shall be loved of my Father, and I will love him, and will manifest myself to him.” The witnesses trace the chain that leads from obedient love to the manifestation of Christ. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the “successive degrees” (Ellicott): first, to “have” and “keep” the commandments — not merely the external letter but the word “written on his heart by the finger of the Spirit” (Gill) — which is the proof of love; then, that such a one “shall be loved of my Father,” and loved of the Son; and finally, that Christ “will manifest myself to him.” Robertson notes the emphatic “he it is that loveth me,” and the future “I will manifest” (emphanisō): “the Unseen and Risen Christ will be a real and spiritual Presence to the obedient and loving believer.” Barnes sums the union the verse reveals: love produces obedience; the obedient are loved by Father and Son alike, “showing that there is a union between the Father and the Son.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, marks why Christ so often repeats that love is proved by obedience: “that they may not turn aside from this object; for there is nothing to which we are more prone than to slide into a carnal affection, so as to love something else than Christ under the name of Christ” (cf. 2 Corinthians 5:16). The witnesses thus find in the verse a precious promise hung upon a searching condition. The promise is the self-manifestation of Christ — not to the world, but to the loving and obedient disciple, an inward revealing of himself by the Spirit that is sweeter than any outward vision. The condition is a love that keeps his commandments. So the verse joins the warmth of intimacy to the rigour of obedience: it is to those who love Christ enough to obey him that he discloses himself, and the keeping of his word is the path to the knowing of his presence.

The witnesses dwell on the sweetness of the closing promise. To the obedient and loving disciple Christ pledges to ‘manifest himself’ — an inward, spiritual revealing that the world cannot share, more precious than any sight of the eyes. So the verse sets before every believer the highest of incentives to loving obedience: not merely the approval of God, but the self-disclosure of Christ to the soul that keeps his word. The path of obedience is the path on which the Lord makes himself known.
22:
“Judas saith unto him, not Iscariot, Lord, how is it that thou wilt manifest thyself unto us, and not unto the world?” The witnesses identify the questioner and read his puzzlement. The grammatical-historical commentators note carefully that this is “Judas, not Iscariot” — Augustine and Gill identify him as Judas Lebbaeus, surnamed Thaddaeus, “the same with Jude the apostle, the author of the epistle… read among the canonical Scriptures.” His question arises from the promise of v.21: how can Christ manifest himself to the disciples “and not unto the world”? Like the others, Judas still half-expects a public, visible manifestation — the open glory of a Messiah revealed to all (Ellicott, Gill) — and cannot grasp a self-disclosure that the world will not share. The Eastern Chrysostom notes that “some of these things were obscure, and not understood by the disciples,” so honest questions kept breaking in.

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, distinguishes the two manifestations the discourse holds together: “the abode He promised them hereafter is altogether different from this present abode He now speaks of. The one is spiritual and inward; the other outward, and perceptible to the bodily sight.” Judas confuses the inward, spiritual revealing Christ now promises with the outward, public manifestation he expected. Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find in the question the same fault that ran through the disciples’ thinking — the lingering hope of an earthly, visible kingdom. The witnesses thus read the verse as the foil to Christ’s answer in v.23. Judas’s honest perplexity — why us and not the world? — draws from Christ the explanation that the manifestation he promises is moral and spiritual, given to those who love and obey, by the Father and the Son coming to make their abode in them. The disciple cannot yet conceive a kingdom that comes not by outward show but by inward indwelling; and his question opens the way for the deepest promise of the discourse.

The witnesses observe that Judas’s difficulty is the difficulty of every mind bound to the visible. He cannot conceive how a King should reveal himself to a few and not to all, because he thinks of manifestation as outward display. Christ’s answer will teach him, and us, that the deepest revelation of God is not a spectacle for the world but an indwelling in the heart of love. The question, honestly asked, becomes the occasion of the discourse’s richest promise.
23:
“Jesus answered and said unto him, If a man love me, he will keep my words: and my Father will love him, and we will come unto him, and make our abode with him.” The witnesses take this as Christ’s answer to Judas (v.22) — the manifestation he promises is inward, given to the loving and obedient. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the repeated condition: “if a man love me, he will keep my words,” which the world, “in its unbelief and rejection of Christ’s words, and without the spirit of love, could not receive” (Ellicott). The promise to such is the indwelling of God himself: Robertson notes the future “we will come” (the Father and the Son), “not at the judgment, but here and now,” and “make our abode” (monē, the same word as the “mansions” of v.2) — so that the heart of the believer becomes the dwelling-place of God. Barnes describes the coming as “the manifestation of pardon, peace of conscience, and joy in the Holy Ghost,” God showing himself “as a Father and Friend.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, guards the order of grace: “the love of God to us is not placed in the second rank, as if it came after our piety as the cause of that love,” but is the spring of all; the obedience of believers assures them “that the grace of God dwelleth in” them, and they may “every day receive additions” of his gifts. Wesley marvels at the largeness of the promise — “such a manifestation of the Divine presence and love, that the former in justification is as nothing in comparison of it.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the crown of the chapter’s teaching on the indwelling. To Judas’s question — why us and not the world? — Christ answers that the manifestation is moral and spiritual: it is given to those who love and keep his word, and it is nothing less than the Father and the Son coming to make their home in the heart. The kingdom comes not by outward show but by this inward abode of God in the loving, obedient believer.
24:
“He that loveth me not keepeth not my sayings: and the word which ye hear is not mine, but the Father’s which sent me.” The witnesses take this as the dark obverse of v.23 — why the world cannot receive the manifestation. The grammatical-historical commentators read the contrast: as love keeps Christ’s words, so the want of love shows itself in not keeping them. Ellicott explains that “he that loveth not Christ keepeth not His word, and that word is the Father’s,” so that in rejecting Christ’s word the world “has rejected the love of God which is revealed in the Son, and has Himself closed the channels of communion.” Clarke draws the sharp inference that “the man who is not obedient to the testimonies of Christ does not love him,” recalling Paul’s “if any man love not the Lord Jesus Christ, let him be Anathema” (1 Corinthians 16:22). Robertson notes the present participle “the one who keeps on not loving me,” and the weighty claim that the word rejected “is not mine, but the Father’s.”

Gill adds that this applies to “external professors,” who keep Christ’s commands outwardly but “not from a principle of love,” and so in truth do not keep them at all. Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the verse as a steadying admonition: since believers are “mixed with unbelievers in the world,” and “must be agitated by various storms,” Christ warns them “that they may not be drawn away by bad examples” — “there will always be some who despise me and my doctrine, but as for you, preserve constantly to the end the grace which you have received.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both an explanation and a warning. The world’s inability to receive Christ’s manifestation is no arbitrary exclusion but the fruit of its own unbelief: not loving him, it will not keep his word — the word that is the Father’s — and so shuts itself out from the indwelling promised to love. And the disciples are warned that the unbelief of the many must not draw them from the obedience of love.
25:
“These things have I spoken unto you, being yet present with you.” The witnesses read this as a pause and a transition — from Christ’s own teaching while present to the Spirit’s teaching after his departure. The grammatical-historical commentators note the perfect tense “have I spoken” (lelalēka, Robertson, “for permanent keeping”) and the participle “while abiding with you,” marking that Christ is about to leave and to hand his teaching over to another Teacher. Ellicott: “He had been speaking… words which they found it hard to understand. He now pauses in His teaching, and proceeds to tell them of the Holy Spirit who should interpret His words to them.” Henry sums the comfort: the disciples should be “under the tuition of his Spirit,” who would impress on them the lessons Christ had taught “being yet present.”

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, observes that the indwelling promised joins all three divine persons: the Father and Son make their abode (v.23), and the Spirit too dwells in them (v.17), “by which we understood that the divine Trinity dwelleth together” in the believer. Calvin, for the Reformed, draws encouragement from the verse: Christ adds it “that they may not despair, though they may have profited less than they ought,” for he had now “scattered a seed of doctrine, which lay hidden, and, as it were, suffocated in the disciples,” to be quickened later by the Spirit. The witnesses thus find in the verse the hinge between the two phases of revelation. While present in the flesh, Christ has sown the truth; much of it the disciples cannot yet grasp. But the seed is not lost; the Spirit, whom the next verse promises, will bring it to life and remembrance. So the verse both closes Christ’s direct instruction and opens the promise of the Comforter who will complete it — that the words spoken now, half-understood, may be fully known hereafter.

The witnesses note the gentle realism of the verse. Christ does not pretend the disciples have understood all he has said; he acknowledges that he has spoken these things while still with them, and that much remains dark. But far from leaving them to their confusion, he points them forward to the Teacher who will make all plain. The verse thus comforts every learner slow to grasp the things of God, assuring him that the seed sown in obscurity will be brought to light by the Spirit in its time.
26:
“But the Comforter, which is the Holy Ghost, whom the Father will send in my name, he shall teach you all things, and bring all things to your remembrance, whatsoever I have said unto you.” The witnesses unfold the Spirit’s twofold teaching office and his procession. The grammatical-historical commentators render the title “the Advocate” or “Comforter” (paraklētos), now expressly named “the Holy Ghost” (a full title rare in John, Ellicott). On the sending, Robertson notes that “the Father will send the Holy Spirit… but so will the Son” (John 15:26; 20:22), “no contradiction in this relation of the Persons in the Trinity.” The Spirit’s work is twofold: to “teach you all things” — all, Barnes and Wesley qualify, “needful for them… in the apostolic office” and “for their salvation” — and to “bring all things to your remembrance,” the inspiration by which the apostles would recall and record Christ’s words. Lightfoot notes that to be “led into all truth” was peculiarly “the apostles’ lot,” though every believer is led into all truth “necessary for himself and his own happiness.”

Clarke draws the comfort for the disciples: where Christ’s sayings seemed obscure, the Spirit “will take away all your doubts… and give you a perfect understanding,” and this Spirit “ye shall shortly receive.” Cyril of Jerusalem adds, from the Acts, that the Spirit truly “discourses and speaks,” guiding Philip and the apostles. The witnesses thus find in the verse the charter of the Spirit’s teaching ministry and, with it, of the New Testament Scriptures. The Comforter, sent by the Father in Christ’s name, would both teach the apostles the things they could not yet bear (John 16:12) and remind them of all that Christ had said, so that their preaching and writing would be no mere human memory but the Spirit’s own work. The departing Lord thus leaves his church not without a teacher: in his stead comes the Spirit of truth, to complete his instruction and to keep his words alive in the church for ever.

The witnesses draw the abiding lesson that the church is never left teacherless. When the Master departs in the flesh, the Spirit comes to abide; what Christ began to teach, the Spirit completes. And the promise that the Spirit would bring Christ’s words to remembrance assures us that the apostolic witness, preserved in the Scriptures, is no fallible recollection but the very work of God, keeping the words of Jesus alive and pure for his people through every age.
27:
“Peace I leave with you, my peace I give unto you: not as the world giveth, give I unto you. Let not your heart be troubled, neither let it be afraid.” The witnesses receive this as Christ’s parting bequest, and dwell on the peace that is uniquely his. The grammatical-historical commentators note the form of an Oriental farewell — “Shalom! Shalom!”, the common greeting at meeting and parting (Ellicott, Clarke) — but insist it is here “much more than a mere form” (Barnes): it comes “from Him who had power to make peace and to confer it” (Ephesians 2:15). Gill calls it Christ’s “last will and testament,” bequeathing “the best legacy he could leave them.” Robertson marks “my peace” (tēn emēn) as “Christ’s bequest… spiritual peace such as only Christ can give,” and notes the contrast “not as the world giveth”: the world’s peace is empty wishing or outward calm, but Christ gives the real, abiding tranquillity of reconciliation with God (cf. Luke 2:14).

The fathers dwell on the consolation. Chrysostom notes that after the farewell word “Peace I leave with you,” Christ at once consoles the “love and fear” uppermost in the disciples — “let not your heart be troubled, neither let it be afraid.” Origen finds an image of why such peace is given now: as a house meant to stand is built “in fine weather and in calm,” so the disciples are settled in peace before the storm of the Passion, that they may stand. Calvin, for the Reformed, contrasts the world’s peace — mere “prosperity… which men wish for each other” — with Christ’s, which is inward and lasting. The witnesses thus find in the verse the sum of the chapter’s consolation, framed by the same words with which it opened (v.1): against every trouble and fear, Christ leaves his own peace. It is not the world’s peace of circumstance, which fails when circumstances fail, but the peace of a heart reconciled to God and resting in Christ, which abides through the cross and beyond it. This is the legacy of the departing Lord — his own peace, bequeathed to his troubled disciples.
28:
“Ye have heard how I said unto you, I go away, and come again unto you. If ye loved me, ye would rejoice, because I said, I go unto the Father: for my Father is greater than I.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s appeal that love should rejoice at his glorification, and they wrestle with the great clause “my Father is greater than I.” The grammatical-historical commentators explain the appeal: the disciples grieve at his going, but a love that sought his good rather than its own would “rejoice, because I go unto the Father” — to glory and exaltation, his work accomplished. On “the Father is greater than I,” the orthodox witnesses are unanimous that this speaks not of the Son’s nature but of his state and office. Christ speaks as the Mediator, “in the form of a servant,” humbled in the flesh; in his humanity and his present low estate the Father is greater, though in his divine essence the Son is equal to the Father (cf. John 10:30; Philippians 2:6).

The Latin Hilary frames the saying carefully against Arian misuse: “if the Father is greater by virtue of giving, is the Son less by confessing the gift? The giver is the greater, but He to whom unity with that giver is given, is not the less.” The Eastern Chrysostom reads it as accommodation to the disciples’ weakness: they “only knew the great power of the Father,” so Christ comforts them that he goes to One greater than the dangers they feared. Calvin, for the Reformed, takes the comparison to be “not of natures” but of the Son’s present condition — the lowliness of his earthly state set against the heavenly glory he goes to share. The witnesses thus find in the verse both an exhortation and a Christological key. The exhortation is that genuine love rejoices in Christ’s exaltation more than it grieves at his departure. The key is that “the Father is greater than I” must be read of the incarnate Son in his humiliation, not of any inequality of Godhead — the verse a favourite of heretics, but rightly understood, no denial of the Son’s deity but a confession of his self-emptying obedience, soon to be crowned with glory at the Father’s side.
29:
“And now I have told you before it come to pass, that, when it is come to pass, ye might believe.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s purpose in foretelling his departure and return — the confirming of the disciples’ faith. The grammatical-historical commentators note the same principle stated earlier of the betrayal (John 13:19): by telling them beforehand what would happen, Christ ensures that the events, when they come, will not shake but strengthen their belief. The fulfilment of his predictions — his death, resurrection, ascension, and the gift of the Spirit — would prove him to be all he claimed, so that the very things that might have overwhelmed their faith become, foreseen and foretold, its confirmation (Gill, Barnes, Ellicott). Augustine, for the Latin fathers, marks the tender care in this forewarning, by which the Lord prepares his own for trials he knows are coming.

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the abiding use of the principle. Faith is not weakened but established when it sees the word of Christ verified by the event; the disciple who has been told beforehand is armed against the offence of what would otherwise dismay him. The witnesses thus find in the verse a window on Christ’s pastoral wisdom. He does not spare his disciples the knowledge of his going, hard as it is to hear, but tells them plainly, that when it comes they may remember and believe. The foretelling is itself an act of love: it turns the coming sorrow into a ground of faith, so that the cross and the parting, when they arrive, shall not surprise them into despair but confirm them in the conviction that their Master is indeed the Christ, who knew and foretold all that befell him.

The witnesses note that this method runs through the whole farewell discourse. Again and again Christ tells the disciples beforehand what is to come, that the event may confirm rather than confound them. So the verse models a settled trust: when the dark things foretold arrive, the believer is to remember that his Lord knew and named them, and to find in their very fulfilment a fresh proof of his truth. Foreknowledge shared in love becomes faith’s anchor in the storm.
30:
“Hereafter I will not talk much with you: for the prince of this world cometh, and hath nothing in me.” The witnesses note both the nearness of the Passion and the great claim of Christ’s sinlessness. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that “the prince of this world” is Satan (cf. John 12:31; John 16:11), now “coming” through Judas and the arrest to do his worst against Christ. But, Christ says, he “hath nothing in me” — Robertson renders “has no claim on me,” for there is in the sinless Son nothing on which Satan’s power can fasten, no sin to give the accuser a hold (Barnes, Ellicott). The “I will not talk much with you” marks that the time for teaching is almost over, the hour of conflict at hand; what remains will be the wordless work of the cross.

The witnesses dwell on the force of “hath nothing in me.” Death has a claim on sinners, and Satan a power over them, because of sin; but Christ, being without sin, owes nothing to death and offers no foothold to the devil. If he goes to the cross, it is not because Satan can compel him, nor because his own guilt requires it, but freely, in love and obedience (as v.31 will say). Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find here a guard upon the doctrine of the atonement: Christ dies not for his own sin — he has none — but, the sinless One, in the room of sinners. The witnesses thus read the verse as the prelude to the Passion and the proof of its meaning. The prince of this world advances against the one man over whom he has no rightful power; and the very fact that Satan has “nothing in” Christ shows that his coming death is no defeat wrung from him by guilt or the devil, but a willing, sinless self-offering — the Lamb without blemish going to the slaughter of his own accord.

The witnesses press the comfort hidden in the claim. Because Satan has nothing in Christ, his coming death is not a victory of the powers of darkness but their undoing; the prince of this world advances only to be cast out (12:31). And for the believer the verse holds a promise — that union with the sinless Christ is the ground on which the accuser’s hold is broken, so that those in him need not fear the one who has nothing in their Lord.
31:
“But that the world may know that I love the Father; and as the Father gave me commandment, even so I do. Arise, let us go hence.” The witnesses close the chapter on the voluntariness of Christ’s death and the call to depart. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that Christ goes to the cross not under Satan’s compulsion (v.30) but to show the world “that I love the Father,” doing “as the Father gave me commandment.” His death is the supreme act of loving obedience, freely rendered, that the world might see the Son’s love for the Father in his willing submission to the Father’s will (Gill, Barnes, Ellicott). Robertson and the others note that this answers the misunderstanding the cross would seem to create: Christ is not overpowered, but obedient, laying down his life of his own accord (cf. John 10:18).

On “Arise, let us go hence,” the commentators observe that Christ now rises to leave the upper room and go toward Gethsemane and the cross. Some note the difficulty that the discourse of chapters 15–17 follows: a few suppose the company rose and continued the teaching on the way or standing, while others take “let us go hence” as spoken in resolve, the further words uttered before they actually departed. Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the words as Christ’s ready advance to meet death, not fleeing but going forth to it. The witnesses thus find in the verse the seal of the chapter and the turning toward the Passion. All Christ has said — the comfort, the promises, the peace — is now gathered into a single act of obedient love: he goes to the cross to show the world his love for the Father, and he goes willingly, rising with the word “Arise, let us go hence.” The consolation of the discourse ends in the movement toward the very death that occasioned it, undertaken not in dread but in love and free obedience to the Father’s command.

The witnesses find in the closing words the keynote of the Passion. Christ goes to his death not as a victim but as the willing Son, that the world may know his love for the Father. The cross is thus framed from the first as an act of obedience and love, freely chosen; and the call ‘Arise, let us go hence’ shows the Saviour advancing to meet it, undismayed. The chapter that began ‘let not your heart be troubled’ ends with the untroubled Lord rising to go forth to the very trouble he has soothed in his disciples.
